original_hypothesis.title: Banning Vault Address Causes Permanent Loss of In-Flight Bridged Tokens
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If the watchdog bans an address (e.g., ERC20Vault) during an emergency, processMessage immediately marks any in-flight messages to that address as DONE and refunds the ETH value. However, the token transfer payload is ignored. Because the status is DONE (not FAILED), the users cannot recall their tokens on the source chain.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While banning is an intended emergency feature, marking token bridge messages as DONE without executing the token mint/release permanently destroys user funds. The tokens are burned/locked on the source chain with no corresponding mint/release on the destination chain, and the recall mechanism is permanently disabled for these messages.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addressBanned[_message.to] marks message DONE without executing payload

preconditions: There are in-flight token bridge messages targeting that vault

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Status set to DONE instead of FAILED when target is banned
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit, but catastrophic loss of user funds during emergency protocol actions

economic_questions: Should banned addresses result in a FAILED status to allow recalls?
How many tokens could be in-flight during an emergency ban?
validation_requirements: Simulate a vault ban with pending messages
Attempt to recall the messages on the source chain

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Severe loss of trust and funds during crisis response

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All in-flight tokens at the time of the ban

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Analyze state transitions for banned addresses

validation_result.impact_summary: When the watchdog bans a vault address, all in-flight token bridge messages targeting that vault are marked DONE without executing the token mint/release. The DONE status prevents recall on the source chain, permanently destroying user tokens.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The processMessage function in Bridge.sol groups banned addresses together with address(0), address(this), and signalService in a single code path that sets the message status to DONE and refunds only _message.value (ETH). The token transfer payload encoded in _message.data is never executed (no call to onMessageInvocation on the vault). On the source chain, tokens were already burned (bridged tokens) or locked (canonical tokens) when sendToken was called. The recallMessage function requires proving that the destination chain emitted a FAILED signal, but _updateMessageStatus only sends the failure signal when status == FAILED, not DONE. Since the message is DONE, no failure signal exists, and recallMessage will revert with B_NOT_FAILED. The tokens are permanently locked/burned on the source chain with no corresponding mint/release on the destination chain and no recall path available.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible in two code sections: (1) processMessage marking banned addresses as DONE at Bridge.sol lines ~216-221, and (2) _updateMessageStatus only sending failure signals for FAILED status. The combination makes token recovery impossible.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When _message.to is a banned vault address, the status is set to DONE and only _message.value (ETH) is refunded. The token payload in _message.data is never executed, so no tokens are minted/released on the destination chain. Setting DONE instead of FAILED permanently prevents recall on the source chain.

The failure signal (signalForFailedMessage) is only sent when status is FAILED. When status is DONE (as set for banned addresses), no failure signal is emitted. This means recallMessage on the source chain can never prove message failure, permanently blocking token recovery.

recallMessage requires proving that the failure signal exists on the destination chain. Since processMessage set the status to DONE (not FAILED) for banned addresses, no failure signal was sent, and this proof will always fail. The source chain message status remains NEW but can never be recalled.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls ERC20Vault.sendToken on source chain - tokens are burned/locked, bridge message sent
2. Watchdog calls banAddress(vaultAddr, true) on destination chain - addressBanned[vaultAddr] = true
3. Relayer calls processMessage on destination chain with the in-flight message
4. processMessage checks addressBanned[_message.to] == true at line ~216
6. _updateMessageStatus sets status to DONE, does NOT send failure signal (only sent for FAILED)
7. ETH fee and value are refunded to relayer and refundTo respectively
8. On source chain, user attempts recallMessage but cannot prove failure signal - B_NOT_FAILED revert
9. Tokens remain permanently locked/burned on source chain with no recovery path
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability exists in processMessage's handling of banned addresses. At Bridge.sol ~216-221, banned addresses are handled identically to address(0)/address(this)/signalService by setting status to DONE. This is incorrect for vault addresses because: (a) the token payload in _message.data is silently discarded, (b) DONE status prevents any retry mechanism, and (c) no failure signal is emitted, permanently blocking recallMessage on the source chain. The correct behavior would be to set status to FAILED (or RETRIABLE) for banned addresses, allowing users to either retry after the ban is lifted or recall their tokens on the source chain.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The banAddress function is available on all deployments via the bridge_watchdog role.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) User bridges 1000 USDC from L1 to L2 via ERC20Vault.sendToken. Tokens are locked in L1 ERC20Vault. (2) An emergency occurs and the bridge_watchdog bans the L2 ERC20Vault address. (3) A relayer processes the in-flight message on L2. processMessage sees addressBanned[erc20Vault] == true, sets status to DONE, refunds only the ETH value. The 1000 USDC remain locked in L1 ERC20Vault. No bridged USDC are minted on L2. (4) User tries to recall on L1 but cannot prove the message failed (it's DONE, not FAILED). The 1000 USDC are permanently lost. This affects ALL in-flight token bridge messages at the time of the ban.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. During an emergency ban of a vault address, all in-flight token bridge messages are permanently destroyed. Tokens locked/burned on the source chain cannot be recovered. The impact scales with the number of in-flight messages and their values at the time of the ban. This is particularly dangerous because banning is an emergency response mechanism, so it likely coincides with high-volume/high-value situations. The defect turns a protective measure into a fund-destroying one.
recommended_mitigations: Change the banned address handling in processMessage to set status to FAILED instead of DONE, enabling recall on the source chain: _updateMessageStatus(msgHash, Status.FAILED); This will emit the failure signal allowing users to recall their tokens.

Alternatively, set status to RETRIABLE for banned addresses, allowing retry after the ban is lifted: _updateMessageStatus(msgHash, Status.RETRIABLE); This preserves the option to execute the message later.

Separate the handling of banned addresses from address(0)/address(this)/signalService since they have fundamentally different semantics - the former is a temporary emergency measure while the latter are permanently invalid targets.
Consider adding an unbanning + retry mechanism: when a vault is unbanned, previously-banned messages in RETRIABLE state can be retried.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bridge, SignalService, ERC20Vault, and BridgedERC20 on two test chains. 2. User sends 1000 tokens via ERC20Vault.sendToken from chain A. 3. On chain B, call banAddress(erc20VaultAddress, true) as bridge_watchdog. 4. Call processMessage on chain B with the pending message. Observe status becomes DONE but no tokens are minted. 5. On chain A, attempt recallMessage. Observe it reverts because no failure signal exists on chain B. 6. Verify tokens are locked in chain A's ERC20Vault with no recovery path.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers may shift depending on compiler/formatting but the code pattern is definitively present in the processMessage function

It's unclear if this was an intentional design choice (accepting token loss as acceptable during emergency) or an oversight - but regardless, the code is defective as it permanently destroys user funds
additional_testing_needed: Test with different vault types (ERC20, ERC721, ERC1155) to confirm all are affected
Test the scenario where a vault is banned then unbanned - confirm DONE messages cannot be retried
Quantify maximum possible in-flight token value during typical operations to assess realistic impact
